summary:
James Clerk Maxwell's formulation of the equations of electromagnetism in 1865, published as A Dynamical Theory of the Electromagnetic Field, represented one of the greatest theoretical achievements in the history of physics. Maxwell unified electricity, magnetism, and optics into a single mathematical framework and predicted the existence of electromagnetic waves traveling at the speed of light — providing the theoretical foundation for [[event:2615|radio]], wireless communication, and ultimately the modern theory of light.

Building on the experimental work of Michael Faraday, Maxwell translated Faraday's intuitive field concepts into a rigorous mathematical form. His four equations (as later simplified by Oliver Heaviside) described how electric and magnetic fields are generated by charges and currents and how they interact with each other. Crucially, the equations predicted that oscillating electric and magnetic fields would propagate through space as waves at approximately 3 × 10⁸ meters per second — which was exactly the measured speed of light. Maxwell concluded that light itself was an electromagnetic wave, unifying optics with electricity and magnetism.

Maxwell's prediction of electromagnetic waves beyond the visible spectrum was confirmed by Heinrich Hertz's experimental demonstration in 1887–88, which laid the experimental foundation for [[event:2615|Marconi's radio invention]]. Maxwell's equations also provided the inconsistency — they predicted the same speed of light regardless of the motion of the observer — that Einstein needed to resolve in his special theory of relativity in 1905. Richard Feynman called the equations 'the most significant event of the 19th century,' and they remain central to electrical engineering, optics, and telecommunications to the present day.
